context: A first-year writing course sees polished essays that no longer show whether students can evaluate sources or revise claims.
original_assignment: Submit a 1,200-word argumentative essay using three outside sources.
original_learning_goal: Students should practice claim framing, source evaluation, revision, and explaining why evidence supports a position.
ai_offload_risk: AI can produce a fluent essay without showing source judgment
AI can hide whether the student revised a weak claim
Detector-first enforcement may produce false certainty
evidence_still_needed: Two source notes showing reliability/context checks
One revision reflection explaining what changed
Short oral or in-class process check
redesigned_proof_artifact: Claim map submitted before final essay
Reflection naming one rejected AI suggestion or one human revision choice
allowed_ai_role: AI may help brainstorm counterarguments and grammar questions, but it may not invent sources, replace source notes, or write the reflection.
equity_access_check: Students may complete process evidence with class notes and library sources
recourse_path: If flagged, student can show notes, claim map, draft history, and explain one source choice
Instructor reviews process evidence before penalty
do_not_claim: This does not make the assignment AI-proof
This is not legal or academic-integrity advice
boundary_note: Local teaching-design aid only. Department policy, accessibility review, and student recourse must remain human-owned.
